title: Calculate Totals Without Fanout Errors
subtitle: Aggregate line values instead of summing repeated order totals
learning_objective: Return one row per order after joining one-to-many line items and calculate totals at the line grain.
recommended_lesson: Lesson 6: Join Grain, Duplicates, and Fanout
why: Revenue is easily overstated when order-level totals repeat once per line item.
prompt: Return `order_id`, `line_count`, and `calculated_order_total` for every order that has line items. Calculate total from `quantity * unit_price`. Order by `order_id`.
expected_result: 7 rows. Order 1001 has 2 lines and a calculated total of 1200; all other orders have 1 or 2 lines as defined by the data.
build_steps: Join orders to order_items on order_id.
explanation: The detail table repeats the order row. Summing line-level values is safe because each line contributes once.
common_mistakes: Summing `o.total_amount`, which repeats for every line.
Omitting GROUP BY and returning one total across all orders.
Counting orders instead of line items.
hints: Group only at the requested one-row-per-order grain.
takeaways: Always identify which value repeats after a join.
Aggregate values at their natural grain.
reflection_questions: What does one output row represent?
stretch_goal: Join customers and add customer_name without changing the one-row-per-order grain.
starter_sql:
SELECT
    o.order_id,
    COUNT(oi.order_item_id) AS line_count,
    SUM(-- line-level calculation) AS calculated_order_total
FROM orders AS o
INNER JOIN order_items AS oi
    ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
GROUP BY o.order_id
ORDER BY o.order_id;
solution_explanation: The query aggregates order-item values back to one row per order without re-summing a repeated order-level total.
solution_walkthrough: The inner join produces one row per order item.
COUNT measures how many line rows belong to each order.
SUM derives total from each unique line.
GROUP BY restores the requested order grain.
